CURRENT AFFAIRS,

A soiree took place under the auspices of the Loyal
Orange Lodge, No. 6, of Lurgan. We have not yet
heard that any drunken Orangeman stabbed any other

- in connection with this function; perhaps, as it was a
““ soiree ”’ nothing stronger than lemonade was on tap
on the premises. Brother Frank Donaldson, of Dublin,
who, we believe, is in the coal trade, honoured the
fashionable function by his presence. ~ One Brother
Allen was in the chair, and he said: —‘‘ He was pleased
that their good friend Br. Donaldson was able to be
present and if he was in his usual good form they
might expect to hear from him something that would
startle them and that they had never heard bofore with

ference to the loyal minority in Dublin who were sur-
unded with those not of the same way of thinking as

) What! are they getting at Brother

Donaldson and sticking pins in coal bags.

Brother the Rev. Wm. Maguire, in the course of his

harangue, remarked that ¢ Protestants were recognised
as the English garrison in Ireland.”” Further down in
tho report of the harangue we read: —‘‘ Continuing, he
implored Orangemen to act as watch dogs of the Pro-
testant religion, which was in his opinion the best re-
ligion for themselves, the best commercially, the best
morally, the best intellectually and the best spiritually,
he implored them to wipe out the odium that Protes-
tants did not attend their places of worship as their
fellow-countrymen did.”” Well, few of us will feel in-
clined to deny the commercial value of being ‘‘ saved ’’
in this country; and our readers will note that the Rev.
orator placed the commercial value first.

The Rev. R. D. Patterson, B.A., also shouted at the
meeting. We had to smile at his references to what we
may call ‘“saved’’ tames! Our readers will keenly
enjoy the following:—‘‘ If the hiss of the serpent was
heard it was because of the treachery of the so-called
Protestants (applause). Nothing but weakness where
determination was required. Let them look for in-
stance at the case of Princess Ena. Times were changed
when one of the English Royal Family would be allowed
to act as she did, times were changed when the King
would have acted as King Edward the Seventh had
acted.  They might as well face facts whether they
liked them or not, and the fact in this case was that the
present King was false to Protestantism. He swore to
maintain the Protestant religion and the first thing he
does is to advance his niece one stage in the social rank
immediately there is a chance of her marriage with a
foreign king. Then he turned and told his subjects
who were protesting against the marriage—if it could be
called a protest for the average Englishman seemed
fonder of football and cockfighting than his religion—
that he could not help the marriage because the Princess
Ena was a German Princess.”” Pity the sorrows of the
poor old fanatics!

When the Rev. R. D. Patterson, B.A., had con-
cluded, Brother Donaldson, the eminent man in the coal
line in Dublin, ascended the tub. Brother Donaldson’
is a thick and thin ‘‘true blue.”” ‘“ They as Protes-
tants, notwithstanding what they heard to the contrary,
belonged to the ascendancy and he could not hold
patience with the men who thought otherwise and he
would show them why. There were forty millions of
people in the United Kingdom, of which Ireland was an
integral part, and of those forty millions thirty-seven
were Protestants so that he did not think they had
much need to be afraid of the other three.”” Not much
of the spirit of Wolfe Tone or Davis about that gentle
Brother!

A contributor, some months ago, let'in a flood of
light on Cahir of the “ milingtary’” in “Gallant’’
Tip. The Cahir Christy Minstrels are filled evidently
with Nigger Ireland propagandist zeal, for the amateur
niggers—it is more class”’ in Cahir, we presume, to
be a nigger than an Irishman—invaded Clogheen on
Sunday last, and gave a “grand’’ performance. The
programme, we are told, comprised  some of the latest
coon, comic and sentimental songs, side-splitting jokes,
charades, corner gags (whatever they are)) monologues,
and a grand: assortment of conundrums.” It is also
announced on the poster, “The Stump Speech a
speciality.” Ts the following admonition, which ap-
pears on the poster, a side-splitting joke or a ‘corner
gag "’—'‘ Come early or you may be late.”’” A curious
thing about this entertainment by Cahir Niggers at
Clogheen, is that the stage for the niggers was lent by
the Committee of the Clogheen Gaelic League! And
we are further informed that several of the Clogheen

Gaelic Leaguers helped to sell tickets for this Nigger-
Ireland propaganda! We are an abnormal country, as
we all know, but when Irish-Ireland in Clogheen helps

The Demonstration in London on Saturday in defence
of the Catholic—we might say the Irish Catholic—
schools of London, was a big fact and showed what po-
tential strength slumbers in the Catholic population to
be aroused to activity when any counter force threatens
it in the open. “ What Cromwell failed to accomplish
with the sword,”” writes a South London correspondont,
¢ shall we permit Birrell to attempt with the pen. West
Battersea demands Catholic schools, Catholic teachers,
under Catholic control. Hands off the Poplar Catholic
Schools. Following some twenty banners bearing mot-
toes similar to the above, the South and East London
Catholic parents five thousand strong marched off from
the Embankment last evening on their way to join the
demonstration against the much discussed Education
Bill.””  Further on our correspondent says:—“Arrived
at the place of meeting after a march of four miles, we
found that we had been preceded by some twenty-five
thousand others, all unanimous in their opposition to
the latest example of Nonconformist bigotry and in-
tolerance. "’

A Donegal reader writes:—“ Dear Sir—Would you
kindly let me have names of the hundred best reading
books (Trish).”” In the first place, we are not quite sure
that there are one hundred Irish books that could be
classed as reading books. Our correspondent had better
write to the Gaelic League publishing department and
to the other firms that publish Irish books for cata-
logues. As to our making out a list, we think in re-
spectfully declining that work we are as sensible as those
who make it a rule not to play with gunpowder. Sup-
posing that there were only 105 reading books extant,
and supposing that we made an attempt to select the
best 100 out of them we fear that we would also make
five implacable enemies for life.

hide themselves. As our contemporary remarks: —“We
were amazed at the number, for we had never seen an
advertisement of those mentioned either in the trade

On Monday, 7th inst., a meeting of a joint Sub-com-
mittee representing the Co. Council and the Co. Com-
mitteo of Louth met in the Court House Dundal'k,lto
consider a communication from the Inquiry Commission
as to whether the methods, requirements and work of
the Department had given satisfaction in the district.
A resolution expressing disagreement with the consti-
tution of the Department and stating that the county
should get a larger refund from the Treasury .in pro-
portion to the local contribution to Imperial expendi-
ture was proposed, whilst as an amendment the follow-
ing was proposed :— That the working of the Act is
wholly out of sympathy with the people, inasmuch that
it is being put into operation by foreigners who have
no knowledge of the wants, wishes, and feelings of our
people ; that whilst the Act was conceived in the best
possible spirit to grapple with the economic conditions
of Agriculture and Technical Education in the country,
its practical operation has been entirely at variance
with its spirit, and the “ economic sense ’’ of the people
revolts against a continuance of the present methods
of the Scotch plantation of TIreland which euphe-
mistically is styled the Department.” Neither the reso-
lution nor the amendment was put to the meeting, the
consideration of the entire matter being postponed for
a fortnight.

We have frequently asked, what is being done with
the vast amount of money realised at the All Ireland
Temperance Bazaar, and we have not yet seen any
statement by way of reply. We hear privately that
some of the funds are being spent in the Athy locality
on lectures on Temperance. The lecturer is, we be-
lieve, a professor of music, and like Mr. Moriarty, the
Secretary or ex-Sccretary of this “All Creeds, All
Classes ”’ Organisation, he happens to be one of the
“Saved.” These lectures, we hear, are not even ad-
vertised in the local press, nor is any account sent to
the press subsequently. The religious orders, like the
Redemptorists and the Capuchins, do not require any
help from the All-Treland Bazaar Funds to preach tem-
perance and enroll the people in temperance organisa-
tions. Why should this gentleman be paid to lecture
on temperance in a district where the  Idolators’’ are
about 85 per cent. of the population, for we learn that
the audiences at the lecture are almost completely com-
posed of the “Saved.”” Are “all creeds, all classes
to supply the money for employing “ Saved ’’ lecturers
in “Idolatrous’” districts? Do not several religious
orders do that work infinitely better a4t no expense to
the funds of any Temperance Society? We would be

It is perhaps well for Mr. Bennett, a shop-keeper of
Cahirciveen, Co. Kerry, that he does not ply his
business 1 some parts of the United States, where the
Stage Irishman is treated to more kicks than ha’pence.
We have been forwarded a picture post card, published
by Lawrence of Dublin, and printed in Saxony, repre-
senting a Stage Irishman in a state of semi-drunkenness,
cllmgmg to some railings. A policeman is addressing
klm—“ Now then, Pat, move on,”’ and “Pat’’ says,
“ Arrah, lave me alone, sure, it’s only playin’ the Harp
I am.” This post card was purchased at Bennett’s
shop. As we have said it is well for Bennett that it

_is Cahirciveen and not in some parts of the United
States that he does business. The Stage Irish post
card dies hard; all the more reason why the Irish
public should have their attention drawn to the nuisance
in season and out of season.

Mr. J. Grew, J.P., proprietor of the Queen’s Hotel,
Portadown, is responsible for a new and promising in-
dustry in the Co. Armagh. The County Armagh Cider
Co. is a _development of the Boyne Valley Fruit In-
dustry which was established some years ago under the
auspices of the Department of Agriculture. Recog-
nising the opportunities for a cider industry in the
apple-growing districts of Armagh, Mr. Grew accepted
the proposal of the Department and bought the entire
plant of the other undertaking. In order to avoid the
transit of fruit supplies to Drogheda, he transferred the
equipment to Portadown, which, being in the centre of
the fruit-growing farms, is specially -uitable for the
headquarters of the industry. ~We are informed by
those who have tried it that the flavour and quality of
this Portadown cider are excellent, which fact goes to
show that the industry has been started on right lines.
We wish it every success.

According to a correspondent, Queenstown wants a
looking after. ‘‘There is not a place in Ireland that
wants it more,’” he says. So indifferent is the condition
of things in nearly all Irish towns that readers of the
progressive LEADER are prone to think that their own
town, wherever it may be, is the very worst in Ireland.
We have no doubt Queenstown is a poor place from the
Irish point of view, but we are sorry to.say that we
think it would not be difficult to match it. Our corres-
pondent complains of the non-pushing of any Irish
goods, with the exception of porter and whiskey. He
says:—‘‘ There are not many here but Shoneens and
Bungs.”” He relates of a shop assistant in one of the
¢ establishments >’ who laughed when a customer asked
him for a box of Irish matches. Perhaps, some of our
readers in  Coty would let in a little light on the state
of things in the town.

What is the difference between a ‘‘ Lucania ’’, cycle
and an imported finished machine? We understand
that a rumour has been floating about that there is no
difference, except that the former is christened in
Dublin, and that Mr. John O’Neill, the maker of
Lucania cycles, is merely an importer of foreign bicycles,
who sells them as Irish. We are glad to say, however,
that there is a big difference. There is no such thing,
of course, as an out and out Irish-made bicycle.
Patented parts, such as saddles and tyres, tubing, free-
wheels, rims, chains, spokes manufactured in specialised
factories, have, we are informed, to be imported.
But apart from these things, wo understand
that Messrs. Pierce & Co., of Wexford, make the great

~ bulk of the machine. Mr. O’Neill does not claim to be

a manufacturer in the extended sense that the great

 engineering firm referred to is—but he does claim and

with justice that the' amount of labour involved in
turning out his-“ Lucania *’ cycles clearly differentiates
it from a ready-made imported article. He claims
t merely to put the parts together, though that in
olf would represent some local labour, but

practically to do all the processes of manu-
facture that can be performed with manual skill
and light machinery. We need not go into details as
to these processes. Those of our readers who care to see
them for themselves will, we are sure, be \:velcome to
do so at Mr. O’Neill’s premises in South King Street.
‘We might mention, however, that the enamelling, lin-
ing, handlebar and' seat-pillar making in connec.tlon
with “Lucania’ cycles are done on the premises,
and the polishing and plating are also done
in Dublin. The rough rims are plated and
drilled for the spokes which come in lengths and
are cub and fashioned at Mr. O’Neill’s, where, by means
of the brazing hearth, all the parts are also fixed
staunchly together. Mr. O’Neill keeps a staff of men
employed all the year round—an important considera-
tion in the cycle business. In the busy season he keeps
over thirty men at work, principally engaged in the
processes referred to. ~ If he were merely an agent for
foreign machines and sold the same number of them as
he now does of his own ‘‘ Lucania ’’ bicycles, he assures
us he would only require a staff of two or three. The
“ Lucania ’ bicycles are sold at popular prices on cash
or easy terms. Among recent orders supplied by Mr.
O’Neill was an important one from the Dublin Corpora-
tion for members of its outdoor staff.

A London reader of the LEADER, whilst staying in
Thurles for a few days recently, set out to purchase a
felt hat. He asked the name of the best hatter in the
town and went there. This “ best hatter *> had no hats
of Irish manufacture. He went to three others in suc-
cession with like result. At the fifth call he succeeded
in getting what he required at Mr. D. H. Ryan’s,
Hibernian House. Evidently they would want an Irish
Industrial Association in Thurles.

June 1st is the last day for applying for the Free
Scholarships which the Committee of Management of
the Munster Training College (Ballingeary) are offering
to Irish speakers. Six scholarships, one for each county
in the province of Mumhan, are offered. They include
a free course of training at the College during the first
session (July), free maintenance, and an allowance for
travelling expenses. Applicants must give an under-
taking to act as teachers of the Irish language after
their course of training. The examination for the
attainment of these scholarships will be held during the
month of June. The centres of examination will de-
pend upon the number of applicants from the different
parts of the province. The scope of examination is as
follows: —(1) Speaking and literary knowledge of
modern Irish; (2) Method of teaching; (3) Personality,
that is suitability of the candidate for the profession of
teaching. Special importance will be attached to the
suitability of the candidate for teaching, as the com-
mittee wish to secure, if possible, that the scholarships
pe given to those who have, as it were, a natural taste
for teaching. The syllabus of the College, with the
appeal for funds, can also now be had.” Dr. Daly
has been appointed Head Master for the coming ses-
sions; Mr. John O’Keane will be available for one
month as Teacher of Method; Miss Annie O’Reilly
(Macroom) will again have charge of the singing class,
and the Committee are endeavouring, \V}th every hope
of success, to secure the services of Mr. Duu'm\uci Foley,
Mr. Padraig MacSuibhne, (Fermoy), and Mr. Diarmuid
O’Leary as Teacher of Method, Lecturer, and Assistant
Teachor of Method respectively. — The College will
open on July 1st, and all applications and communica-
tions should be sent to the Runaire, Dun na nGaedheal,
Corcaigh.

,  CORRESPONDENCE

WaNTED—AN Irise Insurance Co.

DEAR Sir,—Though the promises already received of
new business to form the nucleus of an Irish Mutual
Insurance Co. are decidedly encouraging, many more
will be required before we get within sight of the
£20,000 to deposit with the Government as security for
policy holders. I trust, therefore, that everyone, with-
out exception, who takes an interest in this important
matter will advise me how much insurance they would
be prepared to effect in such a Company, and about how
much the premium would amount to.

I should be pleased, also, if supporters will write me
the names and addresses of three gentlemen whom they
would like to have nominated as trustees (or directors)
of the Company.

As regards shareholders’ capital, I need not repeat
what I have already written. The great success of
companies that flourish without it in England, Scotland,
Australia, and America, amply proves that it is only
usoful as a means by which shareholders can greedily
absorb profits that in mutual companies belong to
policy holders, and are periodically distributed in fu'l
amongst them. Besides this, have we not in the Na-
tional and the Patriotic, which were, until recently,
Irish companies, two bright specimens as to what pro-
prietary insurance means in Ireland.

Very few people in this country are sufficiently in-
sured, and the majority (I venture to say) have never
even thought of insuring. As an incentive to insure,
we want a company of our own in which we all can have
thorough confidence, and the profits of which shall be
divided wholly, and not in part only, amongst those
insured in it. We want no stock holders to share the
profits of our forethought and thrift, and afterwards
to sell our company, perhaps to the first foreign cor-
poration ' that makes a bid for it.

I cannot quite agree with Mr. T. Galloway Rigg
when he says that the Protestants of Ireland would
prefer to insure in a company, the hed office of which
is in London, Glasgow, or Edinburgh, than in one with
head office in Dublin; but, even if that were so, there
are surely others to be considered in Ireland besides
Protestants.—Yours truly,

Dear Mr. Leapern—I yield to no man inside or out-
sido the Trish seas in my admiration of the great work
you have done since September, 1900. I know it is
Tashionable in the Dublin dailies not to address you by
name. They seem, in consequence, to me the more ridi-
culous when, months after date, they assxqnla.te your
ideas, and present them as original. I.W3‘sh now to
bring under your notice what I trust is stretching
things a bit ’’ on your part. God bless. you for hea.p'm.g
ridicule on the monstrosities of the Irish stage. Ridi-
cule—TI beg your pardon! Your passionate and effective
denunciation of the musical travesties of Irish wit—the
Irish stage song—has, thank God, come to stay. But
is there not a limit to be drawn inside your pointed line
of demarcation—and that, too, in view of the fact that
nothing is being supplied us by the Gaelic League or the
modern Irish school of thought in place of what you
rightly condemn. May I venture to suggest that at our
concerts whilst such vulgarisms as “ Slattery’s Mounted
Fut,” “ MacSorley’s Twins,”” “ Mat Hannigan’s Aunt,’’
etc., should be strictly tabooed, songs such as “Killaloe **
—a clever skit on the French and English languages as
it is; “ Ballyhooley Blue Ribbon Army ’’—a caustic re-

PIORMT UA OUWINNIN.

THE CATHOLIC DEFENCE SOCIETY.

WE were recently present at a very interesting

lecture on Windthorst, the great German lay
Catholic.  Naturally, the situation in Ireland was
touched upon, both in the course of the lecture and in
the course of the speeches that followed.

Windthorst was a Catholic German politician. In
his own self-governing country he was leader of a
Catholic political party. There is no possible parallel
to that state of affairs in the political situation here,
where Nationalist politics are what is styled “ non-sec-
tarian,”” and where we do not enjoy self-government.

As in one great aspect of it, the claim of Ireland
against England is a national one—national in the
sense that those few non-Catholics who are not units
of the English in Ireland share in the national
rights, the political Nationalist Party is likely to re-
main “ non-sectarian.”” For our own part, so few are
the non-Catholic Nationalists, and so distracting and
handicapping are the effects of giving them that con-
sideration which they are entitled to so long as they are
part and parcel of the political organisation, that we
believe had the politicians of Ireland fought as an
avowed and exclusively Irish Catholic body during the
past generation—and an avowed Catholic body would
stand for Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play for all, and
5o be in no way inimical to the rights of non-Catholics
—we believe the politicians would have been more effec-
tive. However, the traditions of Irish politics as *“ non-
sectarian ’’ are so firmly established that we do not look
forward to any change in the immediate future, and
the political machine will continue to do without the
full force of the great driving-power that springs from
the outraged consciences and rights of the persecuted
majority of Catholics.

Were Ireland self-governing, a Catholic political
party would instantly spring up as a necessity, and the
budding Windthorst would have his chance; but so
long as Ireland has her great controversy with the Bri-
tish Parliament, the “all creeds, all classes’’ political
programme will probably remain, and a self-denying
ordinance will forbid the Parliamentary Party, or even
the humblest branch of the United Irish League, from
acting formally as a Catholic body. Accepting it as
practically inevitable that the Party and the organisa-
tion will remain “ non-sectarian,’’ the need for a dis-
tinct Catholic organisation is all the more pressing.
There exists, no doubt, a considerable opposition on the
parb of many of the political Nationalists to the develop-
ment, of a distinct Catholic organisation that can act
openly and formally as such. We understand that

opposition, though, needless to say, we have no sym-
pathy with ib. A “non-sectarian’’ Catholic politician
has no right.or reason to object to a distinctly Catholic
organisation being developed. Indeed, so far as logic
and right go, the compromise by which the Inish poli-

tician, as a politician, who is a Catholic, fetters his
pblitical freedom by becoming “ non-sectarian’’ so as
t0 meet the consciences of the handful of non-Catholic

Nationalists that join the political Party and organisa-

tion, would seem'to us to call for a corrective, alike on
principle and policy, in the shape of the establishment

- of an organisation in which the Irish Catholic politician
could act fully and freely as a Catholic unhampered by
any non-this, non-that, or non-the-other-thing,

: The objection on the part of some influential politi-
cians to a distinctly Catholic organisation is a fact,
and as a fact it must be faced. We would not faco it
by the force of logic so much as by dynamic force.
Push ahead with the Catholic Defence Society, and the
more it becomes an established fact, the more rational
and reasonable it will appear in the eyes of the politi-
cians and others who bitterly object to it so long as it
remains little better than a proposition before the
country. Bven the Freeman’s Journal might relax its
caution, and publish the brief reports of monthly meet-
ings without tacking the word “ communicated ’’ after
them, and Mr. Murphy’s /ndependent might decide
that an editorial word or so in favour of the Society
would do its ha’pennies more good than harm.

Windthorst and his fellows had a fair, open field
that unfortunately does not exist yet in this country.
The people the Catholic Defence Society have to fear
are not the non-Catholics, but the ““tame’’ Catholics
and the treacherous Catholics. The “ tame '’ Catholics
are a timid, a de-Irishised, body, and, in part, a pro-
miscuous collection of social mushrooms that are casily
manipulated by those whose interest it is to keep the
Catholics muzzled and kennelled. If the Catholics
come too much into the open, the Ascendancy and their
Jim Campbells commence to operate, and weird stories
are sent over to the Big Brother, and the Charles E.
Martins, and other “educated’ Catholics, throw up
their hands; the tame F'reeman and the so-called Inde-
pendent join in the cry, and Mr. William Dennehy’s
“ Irish Catholic’’ does ditto. Lord Bung’s Fzpress, and
the libeller of Canon MTInerney, screech, and the only
public force left standing on the side of Tolerance, Jus-
tice and Fair Play is the LEADER, which has to receive
all the cavalry

There is no room for a Windthorst at this juncture.
Napoleon, on St. Helena, was, no doubt, as capable a
soldier as he was on the field at Australitz, but, being
on St. Helena, his powers for soldiering were rendered
ineffective. If there is a Windthorst in Ireland at the
present time, perhaps the best thing he could do for
some time to come is to keep his mouth shut, and wait
events. Leaders help on movements; they can focus
movements, but it is essentially movements that make
leaders; and no leader under heaven could lead a
lay Catholic movement on to victory by means of such
fellows as Charles E. Martin, Esq., D.L. The time is
not ripe for our Irish Windthorst, but the time is ripe
to develop, as rapidly as we can, towards that time.

It has been complained that success is difficult, as we
have not a vigorous Catholic press. But that reminds
us rather of the attitude of those who would postpone
overy reform—temperance, economic and national—
until we get Home Rule. Surely the very existence of
such papers as the Freeman, the Independent, the Cork
Ezaminer, and the Irish News, of Belfast, is a fact in
itself that cries aloud for the development and spread
of the Catholic Defence Society.

If Ireland has a future as Irecland, there is a great
future before the Catholic Defence Society; and _the
sooner those who may be thinking of falling into line,
and the sooner those politicians, and others who oppose
it, recognise that fact the: better. It is wonderful how
easy one gets used to facts. When the Catholic De-
fence Society is made a big fact, and a bold fact, it will
take its place in the estimation of all persons—foes as
well as friends—as a thing quite as ordinary, and as
much to be counted with in the political and social
world, as our old friend, the Nonconformist conscience,

THE LEADER.

May 12, 1906.

JOBN BULL AND HIS BALANCE.

HE last weck was the week of stock-taking in the
household of Bull. * John Bull sat down to con-
sider the profits of his year’s trading, and, if he
grumbled a little at the heavy taxes exacted from him,
he consoled himself by thinking how well able he was
to pay them. HHis population had mcr_eascd by so many
millions; his expenditure on education by so many
millions. No doubt the charges were a little dear, but
after all you can’t stay at the Znglisherhof for nothing,
and the meals are extremely generous. At any rate
the maitre d’Ilotel, Mr. Asquith, had promised to
abolish the objectionable charge for fire and lights, and
to reduce the cost of afternoon tea.

The latter announcement was received with feelings
of limited cheerfulness by John’s travelling companion,
young Patrick Murphy, his ward, the son of an old
trade rival, whose moral and commercial education John
had magnanimously consented to supervise and whom
he was now bringing with him on a commercial tour
round the world. His enthusiasm was less, for John,
though making him a travelling companion, permitted
him to pay his own hotel bills, in addition to such
trifling remuneration as John received for his guardian-
ship. Hence it was that Patrick took a keen interest
in the tarifi for tea, for the six-shilling dinner at the
Englisherhof which John never missed being altogether
outside his means, his stay in John’s hotel necessitated
Patrick’s existing for the most part on a diet of plain
breakfast and afternoon tea, a regimen no doubt intel-
lectually stimulating, but by no means sustaining. But
on the other hand, as Patrick, unlike his guardian, never
had a fire in his room, he would have much preferred
that John should induce the proprietor to maintain the
charge for fires and abolish that for tea, rather than
abolish the former, whilst only slightly decreasing the
latter.

In truth, Patrick did not find touring with John
cither very pleasant or very profitable. It was not
alone John’s bad tempers or his post-prandial profanity,
which in no way consisted with his unfailing heb-
domadal attendance at the English chapel. But what
was really annoying was that, while John insisted on
paying all the expenses and even the drink bill of his
South African and Australian cousins, whenever they
came on tour with him, and behaved with even greater
magnanimity to Miss Snow, the French Canadian,
whom he had adopted, he never thought of treating
Patrick in the same manner. Nor was the tour of much
profit to Patrick in a business way. John had business
interests in Dresden and Belgrade, but visiting these
places did not do Patrick the least good, though no
doubt it sounded businesslike. For John took care to
give him no business introductions and was not over-
generous of trade secrets. And, in any case, as his
father’s business had not prospered during the period
of Patrick’s minority and John’s wardship, Patrick’s
remaining resources consisted chiefly of farms, and there
is not much market for farm produce near the Danube.

But a ward is always ungrateful and Patrick was in
truth, incapable of realising John’s magnanimity, as
John did himself. ‘T treat him as if he were my own
son,”” John used always to say, and indeed he made that
the excuse for thrashing him with severity and fre-
quency in his younger days. “ We stay at the same
hotel, and when I take a day off we climb the same
mountains, share the expenses of the same guides, and
if ho is not as healthy and robust as I am, I am afraid
it is because his father gave him a weak constitution,
poor fellow.”” And John would point young Murphy.
out to strangers and say, “ Look at the poetic, un-
ocarbhly look in that young fellow’s face; look at his
dark eyes flashing out in contrast with his pale cheeks ;
the Murphys are a family of poets, not of traders.”
And often again John would grow magnanimous, gene-
rally after he had thrashed him particularly se:.'erely
and say, “Poor little Patsy, how thin and worn ym’l
look, with your great red eyes. Have a glass of brandy
That will set you all right, and I will order a chop for
your breakfast every morning”’’; and sure enough John
gave him the brandy and the chop, but young Murphy

BYE-WAYS IN INDUSTRY.

IrisHE STONE FOR IRISH PURPOSES.

N the show rooms at 190 Great Brunswick Street,
Dublin, the visitor may inspect the tasteful monu-
ment that is shortly to be transferred to the grave of
the late Michael O’Dywer, of Ballycohey, Co. Tippe-
rary, who died in 1902. The monument, which is in
the form of a Celtic Cross, is being erected by public-
subscription.  One particularly interesting fact con-
cerning it is that it is carved out of Mountcharles (Co.
Donegal) stone, and it has been, we are informed, con-
clusively shown that the famous Monasterboice Cross
was made of stone from the same quarry. The Cross at
Monasterboice was erected in the year 923—nearly
1,000 years ago—so that besides being a thing of beauty
and historical interest, it is a monument to the wearing
qualities of Mountcharles stone. Mountcharles stone
is a sand stone, and it takes polish almost like marble.
The Letterkenny Cathedral is built entirely of it.

Messrs. George A. Watson and Co., Ltd., are work-
ing the quarry in Donegal as well as the Doonagore
quarries in the County of Clare, and we learn there are:
about 300 men working in Clare, and about 100 in
Donegal, so that between the two stone industries there:
are about 400 men employed, which is a considerable
matter in a country like Ireland, where industries other
than agricultire are so scarce.

Mountcharles stone—and this should interest Dublin
stone-workers—can be supplied in the rough state in
Dublin; in fact, we understand that the company pre-
fer supplying it that way. Of course, when the stone
is sent to Dublin in the rough, the Dublin stone-
workers have the benefit of the labour in dressing it.
As we have seen, the firm are equal to sending i eveun
in the finished form of a highly-wrought Celtic Cross.
In the Clare quarries there is a business done in pave-
ment “sets.’”” The firm, unfortunately, had recently
to refuse an order for 200,000 tons of ““sets”’ from the
London County Council. “Set’’ making is a trade in
itself, and it is difficult to get that particular, specialised
labour in Ireland. However, a considerable amount of
labour is being trained, and, no doubt, in due time
the Doonagore quarries will be equal to any demands.
that may be made upon them. The stone at the Clare
quarries is mill stone grit; it is claimed for it that it is-.
a beautiful architectural stone; it is suitable for build-
ings, architectural work, engineering, tombstones,
monuments and memorials, etc.

A considerable amount of these stones is exported,
and, needless to say, of the importation of stone into
Ireland there is no end. There are other representa--
tives of the stone industry in Ireland besides Messrs.
Watson and Co.; we have heard of quarries at Newry,.
and thero is a quarry being worked in Galway. A
drive on an outside car for a dozen mile in almost any-
part of Ireland would be enough to suggest to the ordi-
nary observer that the country should be equal to sup-

I AM sure it will be admitted in legal circles that
Lord Justice Fitzgibbon, Mr. Justice Wright, and
Sir Edward Carson know as much Chancery Law as I
have been able to pick out of Law Books. I need not
labour this point. Therefore they have been acquainted
with the principle of Chancery Law laid down in Tudor
(p- 113), that when the instruments of foundation are
in existence, extrinsic evidence to prove the founder’s
intention can be admitted only when the founder’s in-
tention is expressed with ambiguity, and not clearly
defined in those instruments. Tudor (ibid.) says:—
“In the case of old instruments of doubtful meaning,
it is a settled rule, that extrinsic evidence is admitted
for the purpose of clearing up the ambiguity.”” Tudor
says, moreover, that there must be a clear case of ambi-
guity, and that every ambiguity is not sufficient to
admit extrinsic evidence. For he gives in a footnote
(ibidem), the case of Att.-Gen. v. Mayor of Dartmoor
to illustrate the amount of ambiguity that is not suffi-
cient to admit extrinsic evidence.

The three Instruments of Foundation of the Erasmus
Smith Endowment are in existence. Lord Justice Fitz-
gibbon, Mr. Justice Wright, and Sir Edward Carson
have adduced extrinsic evidence—namely, the letter
dated the 6th of June, 1682, the deed dated the 21st of
July, 1681, the will of Erasmus Smith, and other
things, to prove his alleged Protestant intention. There-
fore they have admitted that the alleged Protestant in-
tention of Erasmus Smith is expressed with ambiguity,
and not with clearness or certainty, in the Instruments
of Foundation. They cannot try to wriggle out of this
conclusion by saying that they were not in any Courb of
‘the Chancery Division when they acted and pleaded in
and before the Educational Endowments (Ireland) Com-
mission. For the several cases of Chancery Law which
they have adduced to sustain the positions they had
taken up proved that they professed to conduct their
«case according to the principles of Chancery Law. Lord
Justice Fitzgibbon has, moreover, expressly stated this
(Rep. 1894, p. 245). But over and above this proved
conclusion of Chancery Law, we have Lord Justice Fitz-
gibbon expressly stating that the alleged Protestant in-
tention of Erasmus Smith has been expressed only with
ambiguity and uncertainty in the Instruments of Foun-
dation, if it bo cxpressed therein at all.  For having
the three Instruments of Foundation before him, he
(Rep. 1892, p. 166) said:—*The question whether
Erasmus Smith had any intention of excluding Noncon-
formist Protestants any further than the laws of the
-day compelled him, is one of difficulty. Further evi-
dence bearing on this point is to bo desired.’”” If “fur-
ther evidence’’ than that given in the three Instru-
ments of Foundation is required to show that Erasmus
‘Smith had any intention of excluding Nonconformist
Protestants from the governing body any further than
the laws of the day compelled him, his intention of ex-

was so characteristic of the old coenobitic teachers,
whose learning and piety still command the admiration

THE GAELIC ATHLETIC ASSOCIATION.

HE position of this great national association, from

a financial as well as an athletic point of view,

is a subject worthy of the attention of every Irishman

who has the interests of his country at heart. Perhaps,

thercfore, I may be permitted, through the medium of
the LEADER, to offer a few remarks on the subject.

I shall first take the financial position. The question
may be at once asked, is it satisfactory? It is a noto-
rious and regrettable fact that the association possesses
no ground of its own, and heavy fees have, consequently,
to be paid for the use of Jones’s Road, and other en-
closed grounds, for important matches in the various
cempetitions. Does it not strike every man capable of
looking facts in the face, that this great association, "
which has received thousands of pounds in gate receipts,
should have amassed, by this time, sufficient funds to
purchase, at least, one ground in Dublin, or some other
city or town in Ireland, and properly equip it for all
classes of athletics in connection with the association?
It is time that the facts were looked in the face, and
that those concerned in the welfare of the G.A.A. should
tako steps to remove the cause, or causes, of what is,
undoubtedly, a deplorable condition of affairs.

The question of the gate receipts is very important.
Is there always a proper check on the gate receipts?
Emphatically no! T have frequently been struck with
the extraordinary arrangement whereby an official of
tho association is allowed to take the gate money, with-
out any tickets being given in return, or a turn-stile
check. It may reasonably be asked, would such an ar-
rangement be tolerated by any body of business men?
It would not, because any business carried out in such
loose fashion would soon become bankrupt.  Would
the officials of the G.A.A., if they had a bungery,
trust their assistants to this extent? I don’t think so.

Want of funds has prevented the association from
purchasing a ground, but what has become of the money
which should have accumulated during all those years?
There must have been gross mismanagement, or the as-
sociation would be in a very strong financial position as
well as have numerous grounds fully equipped.

There ought to be some means of getting over the
difficulty. Is it possible that there is not sufficient
business capacity among the Gaels of Ireland to urge
them to raise enough capital to purchase one good
ground? Thereis Jones’s Road. It is the finest ground
in Ireland, and could be made one of the finest recrea-
tion grounds in the kingdom, suitable for all kinds of
athletics. It would be a gross scandal if the Gaels
allowed this ground to pass into other hands.

Would it be treasonable to suggest that the G.A.A.
should take a leaf out of the book of the Bohemian
“Soccer *’ Club, not to speak of the Irish or English
Football Association? In the former, a few enthusiasts

190

combined, and leased Dalymount Park. They equipped
1t with stands, so that it is capable of holding 10,000
people.  Jones’s Road could be fitted to hold 100,000,
as well as being provided with the finest cycling and
running track in the kingdom. Tho Gaelic clubs in and
around are not wholly blameless in tho matter, and it
is time they made a move in the right direction. The
people would support clubs that catered for their amuse-
ment, and provided a decont ground with proper ac-
commodation. Surely there is sufficient national spirit

IRISH DRAWATIC SPECTACLE IN BELFAST.

‘T HAVE read the article by “Chanel”” on literary

drama at the Abbey Theatre. I cannot agree with
‘him that the reason the theatre is not filled is simply
‘because the average theatre-goer has a repugnance to
things Irish. During a recent week the Ulster Hall,
“Belfast (which is as large a building as the Leinster
Hall used to be), was simply packed for two nights by
an appreciative audience whilst a superb and thoroughly
‘Trish dramatic spectacle was enacted. You would have
said that the whole thing was above the heads of the
«crowd, yet here was the crowd, speechless and attentive
whilst scenes of ancient history and legend unfolded
themselves. The scheme of the entertainment was a

step, and a big step, in advance of historical tableaiix.
We had life and movement (dramatic or processional).
Men came in bearing faggots, and kindled the camp
fire. Warriors, weary from battle, ‘came in and lay
around it ; thero was feasting, and you could see by the
gostures fierce controversy. A soldier went out from
the company, and returning in a little while, held up
something that sent a thrill of horror around, so that
all started up. The light fell on it, and you saw that
it was a human head. Suddenly there was weird, un-
carbhly singing. You were watching the illustration of
an old legendary tale—the singing of Dunbo, edited
not long ago by Whitely Stokes for the Revue
Celtique.

There was no theatrical rise and fall of the curtain.
Light and darkness were skilfully alternated, and the
scene noever changed. The same mountains, the same
shadowy treces, looked down on the flight of Deirdre,
and the forging of the pikes in ’Ninety-eight.

Finally, there came in two clumsy country carpenters,
and erected poles, mottoes and banners. A girl and a
young man rode in on their bicycles.

Gaclic workers in Irish tweeds came bustling around,
then crowds arrived, a priest with a troop of school
children, country folk, trippers, fine ladies, the inevit-
able police, hustling others aside.  There were, doubt-
less, over a hundred people on the stage, and the scene
was a Gaelic Feis opening with a dancing competition.

Well, now, what is the moral of all this for the Abbey
Theatre people? T think they should consider whether
it is right for Mr. Yeats to be always writing, and
explaining that they despise stago accessories. He
comes of a family of artists, and, as a matter of fact,
the staging at the Abbey Theatro is always beautiful,
and it would be possible, with a little judgment, to
equal the productions of the Ulster Literary Theatre in
Belfast. The Abbey Theatre is a perfect success as far
as the production of literary drama is concerned. I
confess to an abject admiration of Mr. Synge’s work,
and all Lady Gregory’s little plays. But I think Mr.
Yeats should stop writing to the paper, and making it
appear that the whole thing is the outcome of some
very peculiar cult. The ordinary public reading his
